subject: Physics
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}_1=(\rho \mathrm{A} \times 10) \mathrm{g} \times 5+(\rho \mathrm{A} 6) \mathrm{g} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\rho \mathrm{Ag}(50+18) \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=68 \rho \mathrm{Ag}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & U_f=(\rho A \times 16) g \times 4 \\ & =(\rho A g) \times 64\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=\Delta U=4 \times \rho A g \\ & =4 \times 1000 \times 2 \times 10=8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:

43πR3=100043π13

Uf=10004πR2T=1000×4π10×10-32T

Initial potential energy:

Ui=4π×r2T=4π×10-32T

Therefore, change in potential energy,

∆E=T×1000×4π10-32-T×4π10×10-32

∆E=4×π×7×10-21000-100×10-6

∆E=7.92×10-4J

subject: Physics
Physics
solution:
Given: $h = 5$ cm, $A_1 = 6$ cm², $A_2 = 3$ cm², $g = 1000$ cm/s²
By continuity: $A_1v_1 = A_2v_2 \Rightarrow v_2 = 2v_1$
By Bernoulli's equation: $P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$
Since $P_1 - P_2 = \rho gh$:
$\rho gh = \frac{1}{2}\rho(4v_1^2 - v_1^2) = \frac{3\rho v_1^2}{2}$
$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1000 \times 5}{3}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}}$ cm/s
Rate of flow $Q = A_1v_1 = 6 \times \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}} = 200\sqrt{3}$ cm³/s

subject: Physics
solution:
Apply Bernouli equation between points $1 \& 2$
$\begin{aligned}
& P_1+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2+\rho g h=P_2+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2+0 \\ & P_0+\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{~A}}+\rho g \frac{70}{100}=P_0+\frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \\ & \frac{5000}{0.5}+10^3 \times 10 \frac{70}{100}=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^3 v_2^2
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& 10^3+10^3 \times 7=\frac{10^3}{2} \mathrm{v}_2^2 \\ & \mathrm{v}_2^2=16 \\ & \mathrm{v}_2=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$
As the tank area is large $\mathrm{v}_1$ is negligible compared to $\mathrm{v}_2$

subject: Physics
solution:
Using the equation of continuity:

$A_A v_A = A_B v_B$

$(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.1 = (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times v_B$

$v_B = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.5$ m/s

Using Bernoulli's equation for a horizontal tube:

$P_A + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_A^2 = P_B + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_B^2$

$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho (v_B^2 - v_A^2)$

Substituting the given values:

$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 600 \times (0.5^2 - 0.1^2)$

$P_A - P_B = 300 \times (0.25 - 0.01)$

$P_A - P_B = 300 \times 0.24 = 72$ Pa

Answer: $72$

subject: Physics
solution:
Initially, the centre of mass of the right column will be at height of 75 cm and the centre of mass of the left column will be at 50 cm. After opening of the valve both columns will have height of 125 cm and their centre of mass will be at height 1252 cm. Therefore,

W=-∆U=-m1+m2gh1+h24-m1gh12-m2gh22

Now, m1=ρAh1 and m2=ρAh2

Putting ρ=1000 kg m-3, g=10 m s-2, h1=100 cm, h2=150 cm and A=16×10-4 m2, we get,

W=1 J

subject: Physics
solution:
The terminal velocity of ball of radius $R$ inside a liquid of viscosity $\eta$ can be written as $V_T=\frac{2 R^2 g}{n}(\sigma-\rho)$, where $\sigma$ is the density of ball and $\delta$ is the density of the liquid.
Hence,
$A$ is correct since $V_T \propto R^2$ gives a parabola on a graph
$C$ is correct since $V_T \alpha \frac{1}{\eta}$ and $\eta$ varies with temperature
$D$ is correct since $V_T \alpha(\sigma-\rho)$ i.e., varies with density of liquid.

subject: Physics
solution:
$$ \text { Let the rate of falling water level be }-\frac{d h}{d t} $$
Initially at $t=0 ; h=h$ $$ t=t ; h=0 $$ Then, $A\left(-\frac{d h}{d t}\right)=\pi a^2 \cdot v$ $$ d t=-\frac{A}{\pi a^2 \sqrt{2 g h}} d h $$ $[\because$ velocity of efflux of liquid $v=\sqrt{2 g h}]$ Integrating both sides $$ \int_0^t d t=-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2 g} \pi a^2} \int_h^0 h^{-1 / 2} d h $$ $$ [t]_0^t=-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2 g} \pi a^2} \cdot\left[\frac{h^{1 / 2}}{1 / 2}\right]_h^0 $$ $$ t=\frac{\sqrt{2} A}{\pi a^2} \sqrt{\frac{h}{g}} $$

subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{v}_1=\text { volume immersed in water. } \\ & \mathrm{v}_2=\text { volume immersed in oil. } \\ & \mathrm{v}_1 \rho_{\mathrm{w}} \mathrm{g}+\mathrm{v}_2 \rho_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{g}=\left(\mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{v}_2\right) \rho_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{g} \\ & \mathrm{v}_1+\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \rho_{\mathrm{o}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{w}}}=\left(\mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{v}_2\right) \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{c}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{w}}} \\ & =\mathrm{v}_1+0.8 \mathrm{v}_2=0.9 \mathrm{v}_1+0.9 \mathrm{v}_2 \\ & =0.1 \mathrm{v}_1=0.1 \mathrm{v}_2 \\ & \mathrm{v}_1: \mathrm{v}_2=1: 1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The amount of work done is equal to the change in the surface energy to increase the radius of the bubble.

The formula to calculate the change in surface energy is given by

 ΔU=2(T×ΔA)   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required work done.

∆U=3.5×10-2×4π(0.2)2-(0.1)2×2 J=3.5×10-2×4×227×0.03×2=2×1.32×10-2=264×10-4 J

subject: Physics
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
For water, the surface tension decreases as the temperature increases. At higher temperatures, the water molecules have more kinetic energy and are better able to overcome the cohesive forces at the surface, resulting in a decrease in surface tension.

So, the height of capillary rise will be smaller in hot water and larger in cold water.

Hence, statement I is true, but statement II is false.

subject: Physics
options: Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
solution:
Viscosity is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow, and gases typically have lower molecular interactions and are less dense than liquids, resulting in lower viscosity.

The presence of insoluble impurities in a liquid can decrease its surface tension. Surface tension is related to the cohesive forces between molecules at the surface of a liquid. Insoluble impurities can disrupt these cohesive forces, leading to a decrease in surface tension.

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Physics
solution:
Mass per unit time of the liquid = \rhoav Momentum per second carried by liquid $=\rho a v \times v$

Net force due to bounced back liquid, $\mathrm{F}_{1}=2 \times\left[\frac{1}{4} \rho \mathrm{av}^{2}\right]$

Net force due to stopped liquid, $\mathrm{F}_{2}=\frac{1}{4}$ pav $^{2}$ Total force,

$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{F}_{1}+\mathrm{F}_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{av}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{pav}^{2}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{pav}^{2}$

Net pressure $=\frac{3}{4} \rho v^{2}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The volume of the bigger drop will be, V=43πR3. If the radius of smaller drops is r, using the conservation of volume,

n43πr3=43πR3⇒729×43πr3=43πR3⇒r=R9

The initial surface energy will be,

Ei=4πR2T 

And final surface energy will be,

Ef=n4πr2T=729×4πR92T=36πR2T

Therefore, the change in the surface energy will be,

⇒ΔE=Ef-Ei=32πR2T=32×3.14×1×10-22×75×10-3⇒ΔE=7.5×10-4 J
